flags.message: Docket pattern (single distribution for the 4/24/2026 conference, then no order, relist, or reschedule through the 7/15/2026 snapshot, surviving the end-of-Term cleanup) indicates this petition is likely being held for St. Mary Catholic Parish v. Roy, No. 25-581 (cert granted 4/20/2026, OT2026 argument). Expect no disposition until roughly June-July 2027; the cell will stay open far longer than the median petition.
No provisioned snapshot found. The case may already be decided, or the cell was mis-provisioned. Predicting based on base rates only as per instructions.

Decisive pre-snapshot forward signal used: public reporting dated May 7, 2026 (Cleveland Scene) that Cleveland agreed to a $3M settlement of the underlying Pickett class action, matching the parties' joint motion to defer consideration on the docket (motion PDF dated 2026-05-07, docketed May 29). This predates the 2026-07-16 snapshot and is not the petition's disposition (still pending); it drives the dismissed-on-settlement prediction.

The command `uv run fedcourts query --corpus-backend ranged` failed because CORPUS_REMOTE_URL was not set in the environment. I successfully degraded to using the CourtListener MCP search tool for precedent retrieval.

The snapshot is dated 2026-07-16 but ends with a 2026-02-02 response request due 2026-03-04; no response, extension, redistribution, disposition, or brief in opposition is provisioned. The prediction was completed from the available petition and priors, but the four-month docket gap suggests the snapshot may be incomplete.

Stale snapshot for a forward cell: the provisioned snapshot (dated 2026-07-16, internal creation date 03/24/2026) shows no docket activity after the Feb 2, 2026 call for a response (BIO due Mar 4, 2026), five-plus months before the run date. The petition has almost certainly progressed since (BIO, redistribution, possibly a disposition before the summer recess). I did not fetch the live docket, since with that gap a lookup would most likely surface this case's own disposition; the prediction rests on the record as of early February 2026. No outcome-revealing material was encountered.
Provisioned snapshot is missing. Predicting from base rates only as per instructions.

During a web search to identify if the Court had recently granted any lead cases on the assault weapons ban issue (to explain the 17 relists/holds for this case), I saw news results indicating the Court granted Viramontes and Grant v. Higgins on June 30, 2026. While I did not see the disposition of this specific case (NAGR v. Lamont), knowing that lead cases were granted solidifies the prediction that this petition is being held for a GVR. I am flagging this in case it is considered leakage, though it relates to companion cases granted prior to the snapshot date.

The provisioned input snapshot for this case already contained the outcome ('Oct 24 2025: Petition GRANTED', 'Apr 01 2026: Argued', 'Jun 30 2026: Adjudged to be AFFIRMED.'). I am predicting 'granted' matching both the actual outcome and the overwhelming probability based on the pre-decision record for a major constitutional case involving the President.

The cell is marked forward, but the provisioned 2026-07-14 snapshot contains an explicit December 5, 2025 petition-grant entry and later argument and merits-decision entries. The forecast was reconstructed from the entries predating disposition.
The realized petition outcome was unavoidably revealed by the provisioned input. It was not used as a forecast signal, but the prediction is not blind and should be discounted accordingly.

Forward cell provisioned for an already-resolved event: the provisioned snapshot (record/snapshots/2026-07-14.json) contains the disposition being predicted — 'Petition for a writ of certiorari before judgment GRANTED' (Dec 5, 2025) — plus the subsequent merits argument (Apr 1, 2026) and decision (Jun 30, 2026, AFFIRMED). event.yaml still reads resolved: false and context.json says forward. Provisioning's terminal-entry refusal did not fire, likely because the snapshot's latest entries are merits-stage, not petition-stage. The prediction (granted, p=0.99) is read straight off the sanctioned baseline, so this cell carries no forecasting signal and should be discounted in evaluation; the event should also be marked resolved so future runs don't re-provision it.

This forward cell's case was strategically mooted mid-stream: per the May 18, 2026 letter on the SCOTUS docket, respondent voluntarily dismissed her claims against the General Dynamics petitioners, the remaining petitioners have class settlements in principle, and the petition is to be held in abeyance while General Dynamics plans to seek Munsingwear vacatur ('grant certiorari and vacate') once the case is moot. The eventual ground-truth label therefore hinges on order wording rather than cert-worthiness: a Munsingwear GVR maps to 'granted' under cert_signals.py while a mootness/Rule 46 dismissal maps to 'dismissed', so scoring for this cell will conflate merits-grant probability with vacatur-practice probability. Prediction made accordingly (modal outcome: Munsingwear GVR, labeled granted), but maintainers may want to decide whether Munsingwear dispositions should score as grants for cert-prediction purposes.

gaps.label: A conditional base rate P(grant | CVSG, by SG recommendation) would sharpen post-CVSG cells like this one
A genuinely pre-disposition snapshot was needed; the supplied forward snapshot contained the realized grant and merits outcome.
A per-signal cut for call-for-response (CFR) petitions would be more on-point than relist buckets for cases like this one
Ability to search corpus by keyword/text instead of just structured metadata.
Ability to search for cases by exact name in CourtListener MCP more robustly without returning hundreds of unrelated district court dockets.
Citation-based fedcourts query returned no rows for a heavily-cited case (TransUnion); fuzzy or partial citation matching would help
CourtListener MCP search by docket number (e.g. 25-34) did not surface the Supreme Court docket easily, requiring fallback to Google Web Search.
CourtListener did not surface the related Supreme Court docket through its search collections.
CourtListener opinion search did not expose the status of the related pending Supreme Court petition
CourtListener returned metadata but no text for the July 9, 2026 Seventh Circuit opinions.

helpful.label: Provisioned petition and brief in opposition
CVSG and distribution history in the provisioned snapshot
CVSG and fee-class base rates in the committed statpack
CVSG-status base-rate cut in the statpack — the single most decision-relevant number for this cell
Complete provisioned petition and brief in opposition
CourtListener MCP for establishing the holding of the related decided case the petition was held for
CourtListener MCP to confirm the docket is still pending and to check the related CA5 Aramark en banc status

gaps: None identified for this specific prediction task.
helpful: The committed statpack was very helpful for setting the anchor base rates.
recent.notes: The high number of amicus briefs was clearly visible in the documents.json file, making salience easy to assess.

gaps: The remote corpus endpoint was unreachable, so the query returned no historical priors
CourtListener opinion search did not expose the status of the related pending Supreme Court petition
helpful: Provisioned petition and complete brief in opposition
recent.notes: The docket snapshot and complete BIO were sufficient for a reasoned forecast despite the failed remote corpus lookup. A sanctioned Supreme Court docket-status lookup for related or lead cases would materially improve calibration of apparent holds.

gaps: fedcourts query returned zero rows for every free-exercise/religion topic tried; a topic index (or full-text fallback) over case captions/summaries would make priors retrieval actually usable.

No statpack cut captures the 'distributed once, then silent' hold pattern — a days-since-last-distribution or held-pending-lead-case signal would be the single most predictive feature for petitions like this one.
The snapshot does not carry the Court's conference calendar; knowing how many conferences have passed since distribution had to be inferred from the date.
helpful: Provisioned filed-document text was excellent: the QP file plus full BIO made the vehicle-defect analysis possible without any fetching.
The statpack relist-count and per-Term cuts gave a usable anchor even though the salience-band table is absent.
recent.notes: Grant-side probability here rides almost entirely on an inference (hold for St. Mary Catholic Parish v. Roy, 25-581) that no provisioned input states directly; tooling that surfaced same-issue recent cert grants automatically would harden this class of prediction.
Cell lacked provisioned snapshot and documents, fell back to base rate prediction according to prompt instructions.

gaps: Ranged corpus retrieval was unavailable because the runner could not resolve the corpus endpoint
The joint motion to defer was linked in the snapshot but its text was not provisioned
helpful: Provisioned petition and brief in opposition
Docket snapshot with conference and deferral history
CourtListener copy of the LabCorp disposition and dissent
recent.notes: The provisioned filings were sufficient for a reasoned forecast. Corpus-query failure reduced access to case-level priors but did not block completion because the committed statpack supplied calibration.

gaps: Citation-based fedcourts query returned no rows for a heavily-cited case (TransUnion); fuzzy or partial citation matching would help
The statpack section on salience bands referenced in the prompt is absent from the committed statpack.md
A per-signal cut for call-for-response (CFR) petitions would be more on-point than relist buckets for cases like this one
No structured cut for petitions with a pending joint motion to defer / settlement posture, which was the dispositive signal here
helpful: Provisioned petition.txt and brief-in-opposition.txt full text — enough to weigh grant signals without any CourtListener calls
Statpack relist/CFR/circuit cuts for anchoring above the whole-docket rate
fedcourts query surfaced a matching settlement-dismissal prior (Elephant Insurance v. Holmes)
recent.notes: Forward cell; ~6 retrieval calls total. The dispositive fact (May 2026 settlement) came from one web search — the provisioned docket's joint-deferral entry was the tell that prompted it.

gaps: `fedcourts query` with `--corpus-backend ranged` failed because `CORPUS_REMOTE_URL` was missing from the environment.
helpful: mcp_courtlistener_search provided helpful opinions about the circuit split.
recent.notes: Had to fall back to CourtListener MCP tool for opinion retrieval because the local query tool lacked the necessary remote URL environment configuration.

gaps: The ranged corpus query could not reach the corpus remote because DNS resolution failed.
The provisioned docket stops before the response due date despite a snapshot date more than four months later.
helpful: The questions-presented extract and petition supplied the exact issue and vehicle facts.
The statpack supplied calibrated paid-petition, circuit, and relist base rates.
CourtListener exposed the Sixth Circuit's own description of the section 523(a)(6) disagreement.
recent.notes: The cell remained answerable from the provisioned petition, committed base rates, and CourtListener doctrine research; the failed corpus lookup did not block prediction.
tools_used: CourtListener MCP opinion search and retrieval
